title: How to Read a Case — Companion Notes
subtitle: Ch. 5 · Case Law Research
intro: You don't read an opinion front to back like a story — you read it for the parts that answer your question. Work through Nash v. Tindall Corp. in both modes, then capture each part in your own words.

fields.label: Why does the caption come first — what does it settle before I read anything else?
Issue vs. holding — how do I tell the question from the answer?
Why does the reasoning often matter more than the holding for my own facts?
What does the citator check protect me from?

sections.heading: The parts, in my own words

fields.label: Headnotes — who writes them, what are they for, and why can I never cite them?
Procedural history — what does the posture control about how I read the result?
Facts — how do I spot the facts the court actually relied on?
Disposition — what is the difference between who won and what rule decided it?
With the tags off, what signposts did I use to find my way around the opinion?
One question I carried into the case, and where its answer lived:
Which courts does Nash bind, and what would I check before relying on it in my own jurisdiction?

footer: Hold the question. Read for the answer. Log what you found.
